author: @rossdavidson5122
text: Hey there! This seems really interesting. I think I'm going to be toying with this during the winter break :'D (gotta get through finals [pchem/adv bchem -- fml]). But I'm wondering: why do you change the solvent system? In the leaves the Mn oxygen evolving complex splits water and allows an input of electrons into psii. So technically you could have a refillable solar cell which is only limited by the amount of water at your disposal. Is there a benefit to this solvent system change?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't know i did it that way because it seemed right to me - i am 100% sure there are better ways of doing it - give it a go and let me know how it works out

author: @lamatrizfractal316
text: Great experiment!! Maybe the chlorophyll requires mild conditions in order to not be denaturalized. there are a los of substances in the leaf so maybe there is a way of separating chlorophyll from the other components(is the only active molecule we are interested... ) It could be reconcentrated using vaccum distillation at low temperature. Or maybe do the extraction with the glycol directly. Congrats for you work.

replies.author: @lamatrizfractal316
replies.text:
Very nice paper http://www.plantmethods.com/content/pdf/1746-4811-9-19.pdf 
explainig how to extract the chlorophyll without denaturalizing it. Possible reconcentration centrifugation.

author: @cyprianserapion2022
text: Awesome! Very promising method to build for solar cells themselves. I suggest again to try it with nettles. Nettles have a very large proportion of the chlorophyll can be extracted easily (nettle tea is after 10 days of reaction a dark green solution without machining) and they are easy to find everywhere.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks mate

author: @thagrit
text: Hey Robert ! I asked you about electroplating on graphenized materials like the scotch bright pad you made into a copper sponge. I was wondering if adding copper nano particles to the graphene ink would allow the electroplating to start on the sponge without having to use the other electroplating solution. The one that uses I think it was copper sulphate and sulphuric acid. Was it the Lacey electroplating process? Sorry I seem to have forgotten some of it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i used a normal acid plating bath - I did think it would be worth looking at DES

replies.author: @thagrit
replies.text: So you think that copper nano particles could cause the lacey process to start plating a graphenized sponge?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
thagrit
I haven't teid it - so i can't say definitively but i would think so - if you do try it and it works can you let me know?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
thagrit
or - better - do a video and i will link to it

author: @thagrit
text: So, Robert ? What is slap of the hand on the bottle top chloride? Sorry but the mike got the noise from the bottle rather than what you were saying.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the two bottles are choline chloride and monoethylene glycol - you mix in a 1:2 molar ratio. But that is only a guide mate and you could try other ratios and see what you get
